source.title: Engineering Bacillus subtilis J46 for efficient utilization of galactose through adaptive laboratory evolution

initial_request.research_problem: Bacillus subtilis J46 grows poorly on galactose (specific growth rate ~0.03 h⁻¹), limiting its use on soybean biomass.
initial_request.known_context: B. subtilis has inherent pathways for galactose metabolism but poor growth on galactose.
constraints: Use adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE) to enhance galactose utilization
quantifiable_goals: Increase specific growth rate on galactose

evolution_trajectory.state_before: B. subtilis J46 grows poorly on galactose, limiting its use on soybean biomass.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can adaptive laboratory evolution enhance galactose utilization in B. subtilis?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: ALE can enhance galactose utilization in B. subtilis.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform ALE on B. subtilis J46 in galactose-containing medium.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Study aimed to boost galactose utilization using ALE and elucidate genetic changes.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design ALE experiment with appropriate medium and conditions.

evidence.quote_or_span: Efficient utilization of galactose by microorganisms can lead to the production of valuable bio-products and improved metabolic processes.
While Bacillus subtilis has inherent pathways for galactose metabolism, there is potential for enhancement via evolutionary strategies.
This study aimed to boost galactose utilization in B. subtilis using adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE) and to elucidate the genetic and metabolic changes underlying the observed enhancements.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that ALE can enhance galactose utilization.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to design ALE conditions that select for improved galactose utilization.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Gradual reduction of yeast extract supplementation will force adaptation to galactose as sole carbon source.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use M9 minimal medium with 2% galactose, initially supplemented with yeast extract (0.11% or 1% at different stages), then phased out; transfer every 24 h.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ALE performed with yeast extract phased out to achieve growth without supplementation.

evidence.quote_or_span: Initially, 0.11 percent yeast extract was added to the minimal medium to boost the growth of J46. By progressively lowering the supplementation, growth without supplementation was eventually attained.
Batch cultures were manually transferred to fresh medium every 24 h.
1% yeast extract was initially added to M9 minimal medium containing 2% (w/v) galactose. After 1000 generations, cultivation was carried out without the addition of yeast extract.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Will ALE produce a strain with significantly improved growth on galactose?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Prolonged ALE will yield evolved strains with increased specific growth rate on galactose.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Run ALE for ~5000 generations and isolate evolved strain BSGA14.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Evolved strain BSGA14 achieved a tenfold increase in specific growth rate compared to wild-type. Significant fitness increase confirmed between 1000 and 2500 generations.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Identify genetic basis of improved growth.

evidence.quote_or_span: The strains of B. subtilis underwent multiple rounds of adaptive laboratory evolution (approximately 5000 generations) in an environment that favored the use of galactose.
The significant increase in fitness on galactose was confirmed between 1000 and 2500 generations of ALE.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Evolved strain BSGA14 shows tenfold growth increase.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What mutations are responsible for the improved phenotype?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Whole-genome sequencing will reveal causal mutations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 63 SNPs identified, with key mutations in araR (H226R) and glcR (frameshift 243InsA).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze allele frequencies over time to correlate with fitness increase.

evidence.quote_or_span: Upon selecting the evolved strain BSGA14, a comprehensive whole-genome sequencing revealed the presence of 63 single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs).
Two of them, located in the coding sequences of the genes araR and glcR, were found to be the advantageous mutations after reverse engineering.
A total of 63 SNP mutations were identified in the BSGA14 strain
Conversely, the frequencies of $araR^{H226R}$ and $glcR^{243InsA}$ mutations surged from 0 to over 90%.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Key mutations identified in araR and glcR.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do these mutations correlate with the fitness increase?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Allele frequencies of araR and glcR mutations increased during the period of fitness gain.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure allele frequencies of araR and glcR mutations across generations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Mutation frequencies of araR and glcR surged from 0 to >90% between generations 1000-2000, coinciding with growth rate increase.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Formulate mechanistic hypothesis for araR mutation.

evidence.quote_or_span: a significant increment was recorded between the 1000 to 2000 generation span, which aligned with the escalation of the specific growth rate
Conversely, the frequencies of $araR^{H226R}$ and $glcR^{243InsA}$ mutations surged from 0 to over 90%.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: araR H226R mutation swept to high frequency.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How does araR H226R improve galactose utilization?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: araR H226R reduces DNA binding affinity, derepressing araE and improving galactose uptake.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test hypothesis by reverse engineering araR H226R mutation.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Low DNA binding affinity to arabinose operon caused by araR H226R; araE highly expressed, improving galactose uptake.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Construct and test araR H226R single mutant.

evidence.quote_or_span: Low DNA binding affinity to the arabinose operon was caused by a non-synonymous nucleotide substitution location in $araR^{H226R}$ close to the arabinose binding site on the C-terminal domain
Due to the $araR^{H226R}$ mutation, araE was highly expressed, which improved galactose uptake into the cell.
Due to the $araR^{H226R}$ mutation, araE was highly expressed, which improved galactose uptake into the cell.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that araR H226R improves galactose uptake via araE derepression.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does araR H226R alone confer improved growth and gene expression changes?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Single mutant BSGALE1 (araR H226R) will show improved growth and upregulation of araE, araB, abnA.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct BSGALE1 via CRISPR-cpf1 and measure growth and gene expression.
evolution_trajectory.observation: BSGALE1 showed improved growth; araE upregulated 3.99-fold, araB 3.45-fold, abnA 2.99-fold.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test araR deletion to confirm loss-of-function effect.

evidence.quote_or_span: The expression of araE (increased by 3.99-fold and 2.88-fold), araB (increased by 3.45-fold and 2.87-fold), and abnA (increased by 2.99-fold and 2.65-fold) was much higher in BSGA14 and BSGALE1
Reverse engineering of B. subtilis J46 introduced mutation of $araR^{H226R}$
The growth profile and galactose consumption of B. subtilis J46, BSGA14, and BSGALE1 in M9 minimal medium containing 2% (w/v) galactose.
Change in gene expression levels of the arabinose metabolic pathways in response to galactose adaptation.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: araR H226R improves growth, but is it a simple loss-of-function?
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does araR deletion mimic the H226R effect?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: araR deletion will result in similar or better growth than H226R.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct araR deletion strain BSGALE2 and measure growth.
evolution_trajectory.observation: BSGALE2 grew slower than araR H226R mutant, unexpected.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Investigate glcR mutation and combined effects.

evidence.quote_or_span: The araR deleted strain BSGALE2 demonstrated a lower specific growth rate than the $araR^{H226R}$ mutant, which was unexpected
The araR deleted strain BSGALE2 demonstrated a lower specific growth rate than the $araR^{H226R}$ mutant, which was unexpected

evolution_trajectory.state_before: araR H226R alone does not fully recapitulate BSGA14 growth; glcR mutation also swept.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What is the contribution of glcR deletion?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: glcR deletion and combined araR H226R + ΔglcR will further improve growth.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct BSGALE3 (ΔglcR) and BSGALE4 (araR H226R + ΔglcR) and profile growth.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Growth profiles of BSGALE3 and BSGALE4 measured in M9 minimal medium with 2% galactose.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze gene expression of galactose pathway in mutants.

evidence.quote_or_span: Reverse engineering of B. subtilis J46 introduced mutation of $\Delta glcR$
The growth profile and galactose consumption of B. subtilis J46, BSGA14, BSGALE3 and BSGALE4 in M9 minimal medium containing 2% (w/v) galactose.
subtilis J46 introduced mutation of $araR^{H226R}$ and $\Delta araR$ (a) The growth profile and galactose consumption of BSGALE1 and BSGALE2 in M9 minimal medium containing 2% (w/v) galactose.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How do these mutations affect galactose metabolic pathway genes?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Galactose catabolic genes (galE, galT, galK) are upregulated in BSGA14 and BSGALE3.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform gene expression analysis of galactose metabolic pathway.
evolution_trajectory.observation: galE, galT, galK upregulated in BSGA14 and BSGALE3 (log2 fold change).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Confirm that double mutant recapitulates evolved phenotype.

evidence.quote_or_span: Change in gene expression levels of the galactose metabolic pathways.
$Log_{2}$ fold change compared to genes related to galactose catabolism of the parental strain

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Gene expression supports galactose pathway upregulation.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does the double mutant BSGALE4 achieve growth comparable to BSGA14?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: BSGALE4 will achieve growth rate close to BSGA14, confirming araR and glcR mutations account for >80% of fitness gain.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare growth rate of BSGALE4 to BSGA14.
evolution_trajectory.observation: BSGALE4 achieved growth rate close to BSGA14; araR and glcR mutations responsible for >80% of fitness improvements.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate strains in application-relevant soybean medium.

evidence.quote_or_span: This study discovered araR and glcR mutations, which were responsible for more than 80% of the fitness improvements of BSGA14 over the J46 strain.
The growth profile and galactose consumption of B. subtilis J46, BSGA14, BSGALE3 and BSGALE4 in M9 minimal medium containing 2% (w/v) galactose.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Key mutations validated in minimal medium.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do evolved and engineered strains perform better in soybean medium?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: BSGA14 and BSGALE4 will show improved growth and enzyme production in mock soybean medium.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure growth and enzyme activities (protease, β-galactosidase) in soybean medium.
evolution_trajectory.observation: BSGA14 protease 65.4 ± 6.5 U/mL, β-galactosidase 20.3 ± 1.5 U/mL; J46 protease 3.4 ± 0.6 U/mL, β-galactosidase 1.3 ± 0.05 U/mL. BSGALE4 also showed improved biomass.

evidence.quote_or_span: the BSGA14 strain exhibited protease activity at $65.4 \pm 6.5$ U/mL and $\beta$ -galactosidase activity at $20.3 \pm 1.5$ U/mL
the wild type strain J46 demonstrated substantially lower enzyme activities, with protease and $\beta$ -galactosidase activities recorded at $3.4 \pm 0.6$ U/mL and $1.3 \pm 0.05$ U/mL, respectively
In addition, the reverse engineered strain BSGALE4 had 3.08-fold biomass than the J46 in 24 h.

validation_methods: Reverse engineering of single and double mutants
Growth profiling in minimal and soybean media
success_verification.final_conclusion: ALE successfully enhanced galactose utilization in B. subtilis J46, yielding strain BSGA14 with tenfold growth increase. Key mutations araR H226R and glcR frameshift account for >80% of fitness gain, validated by reverse engineering. Evolved and engineered strains show improved enzyme production in soybean medium.
limitations: Other mutations beyond araR and glcR may contribute to fitness
Scale-up and industrial application not tested
Mechanism of glcR contribution not fully elucidated
